narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad work and LSAT prep—it's heartbreaking to see your law school dreams hit a snag like this. The admissions model we use to predict bar exam success took a close look at your profile and landed on a 40.6% chance of not passing, which tipped the scales against your application. What stood out most was your Year 1 decile of 3.0, the strongest signal pulling toward this outcome, suggesting an early struggle to stand out academically that the model sees as a big hurdle for bar prep. Your LSAT score of 36.5 reinforced that concern next, as it didn't quite hit the marks that typically predict the kind of analytical edge needed to conquer the exam. Layered on top was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which continued to paint a picture of challenges in keeping pace with top performers, even as other factors came into play. Your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, surprisingly nudged the prediction toward risk in this model's view, perhaps because it expects even stronger academic showings from students with those resources. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 followed suit, not quite lifting you into the safer zone the model favors. Elements like your background as a white female, along with planning full-time studies, added their own subtle weights toward caution, rounding out a profile that, in combination, just didn't inspire full confidence. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances—it's this mosaic of early grades and test scores that made the model wary. But here's the empowering part: law school isn't just about these snapshots; with targeted prep on those foundational skills, students like you have turned similar profiles around and passed the bar brilliantly. You have the drive—your full-time commitment shows that—and focusing on boosting those deciles through stronger habits could rewrite this story next time. Hang in there; this is one chapter, not the whole book.
